title: The Satipatthana Sutta — The Four Foundations of Mindfulness

summary.one_line: The Satipatthana Sutta (MN 10) as a complete contemplative system keyed to the four levels of the subtle body — physical, etheric, astral, and spiritual.
summary.scope: Full text of MN 10 (Thanissaro Bhikkhu trans.) with cross-tradition analysis connecting the four frames of reference to the bhūta-śuddhi of kayagatasati, the prānic currents of vedanā, the vṛtti-observation of cittanupassanā, and the tattva-recognition of dhammanupassanā.

sources.label: Satipatthana Sutta: Frames of Reference (MN 10), trans. Thanissaro Bhikkhu, 2008

body.text: MN 10 opens with the declaration: 'This is the direct path for the purification of beings, for the overcoming of sorrow & lamentation, for the disappearance of pain & distress, for the attainment of the right method, & for the realization of Unbinding — in other words, the four frames of reference.' The first foundation — contemplation of the body (kāyānupassanā) — begins with mindfulness of breathing: 'Breathing in long, he discerns, "I am breathing in long"... He trains himself, "I will breathe in sensitive to the entire body." He trains himself, "I will breathe in calming bodily fabrication."' This practice of kāyagatasati corresponds precisely to the bhūta-śuddhi of the physical body in Tantric tradition — the purification of the gross elements through prāṇa-awareness.

The body contemplation unfolds through six exercises: (1) mindfulness of breathing, (2) postural awareness, (3) full alertness in all activities, (4) the 32 parts of the body, (5) the four elements (earth, water, fire, wind), and (6) the nine charnel-ground contemplations. The instruction for the elements is: 'The monk contemplates this very body — however it stands, however it is disposed — in terms of properties: "In this body there is the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, & the wind property."' Just as a skilled butcher cuts up a cow at a crossroads, the practitioner deconstructs the body into its constituent dhātus — the same analytical method used in Tantric bhūta-śuddhi to dissolve the physical into the elemental.

The second foundation — contemplation of feelings (vedanānupassanā) — trains the practitioner to discern each feeling as it arises: 'When feeling a painful feeling, he discerns, "I am feeling a painful feeling." When feeling a pleasant feeling, he discerns, "I am feeling a pleasant feeling." When feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he discerns, "I am feeling a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling."' Each feeling is further distinguished as 'of the flesh' (sāmisa) or 'not of the flesh' (nirāmisa). This is the direct observation of the prānic currents moving through the etheric body — the felt sense of contraction (pain), expansion (pleasure), and stasis (neutral) that constitutes the prāna-maya-kośa.

The third foundation — contemplation of mind (cittānupassanā) — is the observation of mental states as discrete events: 'When the mind has passion, he discerns that the mind has passion. When the mind is without passion, he discerns that the mind is without passion. When the mind has aversion, he discerns that the mind has aversion... When the mind is constricted, he discerns that the mind is constricted. When the mind is scattered, he discerns that the mind is scattered.' This is the exact equivalent of Yoga-sūtra 1.2 (yogas chitta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ) — the observer-position from which all vṛttis (modifications of the mind-stuff) are seen as objects. The astral body is the field of these vṛttis, and cittānupassanā is their systematic observation without identification.

The fourth foundation — contemplation of mental qualities (dhammānupassanā) — encompasses the five hindrances, the five aggregates, the six sense-bases, the seven factors of awakening, and the four noble truths. The instruction for the hindrances is paradigmatic: 'There being sensual desire present within, he discerns that "There is sensual desire present within me." ... He discerns how there is the arising of unarisen sensual desire. And he discerns how there is the abandoning of sensual desire once it has arisen. And he discerns how there is no future arising of sensual desire that has been abandoned.' This is the recognition of the tattvas — the invariant patterns or 'thatnesses' that constitute the spiritual body. Each hindrance, aggregate, and factor is a tattva to be seen, understood, and transcended.

gaps: Deeper treatment of each of the 6 body contemplations needed
More on sāmisa/nirāmisa distinction and its prānic correlates
Full comparison with Yoga-sūtra vṛtti theory needed
Systematic mapping of each hindrance/aggregate to specific tattvas

issues.title: Systematic tattva mapping incomplete
issues.description: The dhammanupassana section maps hindrances/aggregates to tattvas at a high level. A detailed table matching each of the 5 hindrances, 5 aggregates, 6 sense-bases, 7 awakening factors, and 4 truths to specific tattvas from the Tantraloka system is needed.

issues.title: Missing comparison with DN 22 (Mahasatipatthana)
issues.description: The longer version of this sutta (DN 22) includes expanded treatment of the four noble truths. A comparative analysis is needed.

versions.summary: Initial RO with 5 passages covering all four foundations. Key cross-tradition mappings established: kayagatasati → bhūta-śuddhi, vedanā → prāna, citta → vṛtti, dhammā → tattva.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with five passages from MN 10
